title: Adding NOT NULL column to 50M-row table without downtime

current_state.description: users table has 50M rows. Need to add `subscription_tier` NOT NULL DEFAULT 'free'
current_state.code_sample:
-- naive
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN subscription_tier TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'free';

target_state.description: Column exists, all rows populated, NOT NULL enforced
target_state.code_sample:
-- after migration
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN subscription_tier SET NOT NULL;

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single ALTER TABLE with NOT NULL DEFAULT (rewrites whole table, locks for minutes)
migration_strategy: Phase 1: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN with DEFAULT (Postgres 11+ instant), no NOT NULL yet
Phase 2: Backfill in batches of 10k rows with sleep
Phase 3: Verify no NULLs remain
Phase 4: ALTER COLUMN SET NOT NULL (now fast — only checks constraint)
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Postgres 11+ supports ADD COLUMN with DEFAULT instantly (metadata only, no rewrite).
2. NOT NULL with DEFAULT is also instant in PG 11+, but only for non-volatile defaults.
3. App code must handle both null and value during transition.
4. Backfill in chunks avoids long-running transaction + WAL bloat.
5. SET NOT NULL after backfill is fast (validates constraint).
6. Rollback: column can be dropped if rows fit in app, but plan for forward-only.
rollback_procedure: DROP COLUMN reverts (loses data); test rollback in staging before prod.
feature_flag_strategy: App reads column behind flag tier_v2; falls back to inferring from old column until flag enabled.
telemetry_requirements: Counter: rows backfilled per batch; gauge: rows remaining; log: any NULL after Phase 3.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single ALTER NOT NULL DEFAULT locks the table for minutes; reads block; users see timeouts; eventually OOM.

title: Class component → hooks with state colocation

current_state.description: 800-line UserDashboard class component with 15 instance methods, lifecycle hooks
current_state.code_sample: class UserDashboard extends React.Component { state = {...}; componentDidMount() {...}; render() {...} }
current_state.business_drivers: Hard to extract reusable logic; can't use modern context APIs

target_state.description: Hooks-based, with custom hooks for distinct concerns
target_state.code_sample: export function UserDashboard() { const profile = useUserProfile(); const orders = useUserOrders(); return ... }
target_state.benefits: Reusable hooks; smaller bundle; easier testing

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Refactor and feature change in same PR
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Add characterization tests for current behavior
Phase 2: Extract pure functions
Phase 3: Convert lifecycle methods to useEffect (one at a time)
Phase 4: Extract custom hooks (useUserProfile, useUserOrders)
Phase 5: Convert outer component to function
Phase 6: Remove class
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Tests FIRST — must be able to verify equivalence after refactor.
2. Component-by-component, not all at once.
3. Lifecycle → useEffect mapping isn't 1:1; rethink dependencies.
4. Extract custom hooks based on data dependencies, not by where state lived in class.
5. Each phase a separate PR — small reviews, easy rollback.
rollback_procedure: Each phase deployable independently; revert single PR if needed
feature_flag_strategy: useUserDashboardV2 flag toggles old vs new; default off, ramp 1%→100%
telemetry_requirements: Error rate per version; render count; load time
what_breaks_naive_approach: Big-bang rewrite: 800 lines of behavior change in one PR; subtle bug ships; rollback wipes 2 weeks of refinements.

title: Identifying bounded contexts before splitting monolith

current_state.description: Single Node.js monolith with all features. Team wants to split.
current_state.code_sample: // One package.json, one DB, shared models everywhere
current_state.business_drivers: Team wants independent deploys; one slow team blocks all others

target_state.description: Modular monolith first; microservices only where bounded context proven
target_state.code_sample: // packages/billing, packages/users, packages/inventory — separate modules, single deploy unit
target_state.benefits: Independent test/build; clear ownership; refactoring possible without full understanding

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Microservices by Conway's Law (one per team)
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Identify bounded contexts via DDD event-storming
Phase 2: Extract modules within monolith (still one deploy)
Phase 3: Add inter-module interfaces — internal APIs
Phase 4: Measure: does any module need independent deploy/scale? If not, STOP.
Phase 5: Only for proven need, extract to separate service
Phase 6: Keep DB unified until performance forces split
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. 90% of 'we need microservices' = poor monolith structure, not deployment issue.
2. Modular monolith captures organizational benefits (ownership, parallel work) without distributed-system pain.
3. Network is unreliable; transactions become eventual; observability gets harder.
4. Extract a service ONLY when: independent scale requirements, independent deploy cadence, different team lifecycle, polyglot need.
5. Conway's Law inverted: shape software to enable team autonomy; don't split arbitrarily.
6. Database split is the costliest piece; defer as long as possible.
rollback_procedure: Modular monolith refactor reversible per module. Service extraction harder; bring back as in-process call.
feature_flag_strategy: Route flags for cross-service calls; fall back to in-monolith calls
telemetry_requirements: Inter-module call counts; latency; failure rate; deployment frequency per module
what_breaks_naive_approach: Microservices from day 1: distributed transactions impossible, eventual consistency bugs, oncall complexity 5x.

title: React 17 → 19 incremental upgrade

current_state.description: App on React 17, using deprecated APIs (defaultProps on function components, string refs in legacy code)
current_state.business_drivers: Want Suspense, use() hook, server components

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Upgrade to React 17.0.2 (latest patch)
Phase 2: Enable StrictMode in dev, fix double-invoke surprises
Phase 3: Upgrade to React 18 (concurrent renderer)
Phase 4: Migrate ReactDOM.render → createRoot
Phase 5: Replace defaultProps with destructuring defaults
Phase 6: Upgrade to React 19
Phase 7: Adopt new APIs (use, useTransition, etc.)
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Two major-version jumps in one go = compounded breakage. Stair-step.
2. StrictMode reveals impure renders BEFORE concurrent mode bites.
3. defaultProps deprecation: codemod available.
4. createRoot is required for concurrent features; check that hydration matches SSR output (test-137).
5. Each version has codemods; run them.
6. Test coverage MUST be solid before — refactor wholesale otherwise.
rollback_procedure: Each phase a separate deploy; revert React version + lockfile via git
feature_flag_strategy: N/A — version locked at build
telemetry_requirements: Error rate per version; hydration mismatches; render-time changes
what_breaks_naive_approach: Big jump: StrictMode double-effects surface latent bugs, hydration mismatches, defaultProps suddenly undefined.

title: Strangler Fig: replacing legacy reporting subsystem

current_state.description: Legacy PHP reporting service, 200 endpoints, no tests, owned by team that left
current_state.business_drivers: Can't add new metrics; PHP version EOL

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Replacing all endpoints before any in prod
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Reverse-proxy in front of legacy (Nginx + new service)
Phase 2: New service for ONE endpoint; rest proxied to legacy
Phase 3: Run shadow traffic: forward to BOTH, compare outputs
Phase 4: Cut over endpoint when 99.9% match for 7 days
Phase 5: Repeat per endpoint
Phase 6: Decommission legacy when last endpoint migrated
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Strangler proxy is the seam. Both old and new behind it; route per-endpoint.
2. Shadow traffic catches behavior differences without affecting users.
3. One endpoint at a time = bounded blast radius.
4. Don't promise full migration in fixed time; pace per endpoint quality.
5. 'Replacing legacy' projects fail because they aim for parity-then-replace; do small switches with proven equivalence.
rollback_procedure: Per-endpoint cutover; revert routing to legacy
feature_flag_strategy: Per-endpoint config: percentage to new service
telemetry_requirements: Shadow comparison success rate; latency per service; error rate
what_breaks_naive_approach: Full rewrite: 18-month project, parity untested, surprises in production, users see new bugs in 'replaced' areas.

title: Adding v2 endpoints alongside v1 with deprecation tracking

current_state.description: v1 /api/orders returns flat structure. v2 needs nested customer + line items.
current_state.business_drivers: Mobile clients need joined data without N+1

target_state.description: v1 still works (legacy clients); v2 new shape
target_state.benefits: Better client UX without breaking existing

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Forcing all clients to v2 in same release
migration_strategy: Phase 1: v2 endpoints introduced, alongside v1
Phase 2: Deprecation header added to v1 responses
Phase 3: Telemetry tracks per-client v1 usage
Phase 4: Outreach to top consumers
Phase 5: Sunset notice with date
Phase 6: Remove v1
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. v1 stays until usage proves zero from non-deprecated clients.
2. Deprecation header (RFC 8594) lets clients warn in logs.
3. Sunset header (RFC 8594) with date.
4. Outreach by user_agent identification; coordinate with mobile teams.
5. Don't remove based on calendar; remove based on traffic.
rollback_procedure: v2 can be disabled; v1 untouched
feature_flag_strategy: Per-route: serve v2 to opted-in clients
what_breaks_naive_approach: Breaking change: mobile apps with old version crash; review cycles weeks; complaints flood.

title: Characterization tests before legacy refactor

current_state.description: 800-line generateReport() function, no tests, owned by departed engineer
current_state.business_drivers: Bug fixes risky without safety net

target_state.description: Function refactored into composable pieces with tests
target_state.code_sample: const data = await fetchData(args); const totals = calcTotals(data); const formatted = format(totals);

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Snapshot current outputs across diverse inputs (golden master)
Phase 2: Add black-box tests asserting golden masters
Phase 3: Refactor inside green bar — small steps
Phase 4: Add unit tests for extracted functions
Phase 5: Remove golden-master tests OR keep as smoke
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Characterization tests capture BEHAVIOR not intent.
2. Run function against representative inputs; save outputs.
3. Any refactor that changes output = caught.
4. Refactor in small steps, run tests after each.
5. Once code is broken into testable pieces, write proper tests.
6. Golden masters retire when finer-grained tests cover same.
rollback_procedure: Refactor as separate PR; revert if golden masters fail
feature_flag_strategy: Optional: dual-run old and new for divergence detection
what_breaks_naive_approach: Refactor without tests: subtle behavior change ships, customer hit, revert blamed on the refactor.

title: Idempotent data backfill for 10M user records

current_state.description: Users missing `display_name` field; need to populate from `first_name + last_name`

target_state.description: All users have display_name; new signups get it on insert

migration_strategy: Phase 1: App writes display_name on new inserts
Phase 2: Idempotent backfill script: WHERE display_name IS NULL only
Phase 3: Batch size 10k with sleep
Phase 4: Track progress in checkpoint table
Phase 5: Resume from checkpoint if interrupted
Phase 6: Verify zero NULLs; add NOT NULL constraint
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Idempotent: re-running the script must not corrupt. Filter on NULL only.
2. Checkpoint table records last_id processed; resume continues from there.
3. Batches prevent long-running transactions (WAL bloat, replica lag).
4. App writes the field for new users so the set of NULLs shrinks monotonically.
5. Throttle to avoid replication lag spike.
rollback_procedure: Update is forward-only — rollback wipes the data; restore from backup
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
telemetry_requirements: Rows processed/sec; replica lag; estimated completion
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single UPDATE: 10M rows × WAL = 50GB extra writes, replication lag, app errors during long transaction.

title: Renaming a column in Postgres without downtime

current_state.description: users.email → users.email_address. Old name in 47 places.
current_state.code_sample: -- ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN email TO email_address; -- breaks reads

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Add email_address column, dual-write via generated column or trigger
Phase 2: Backfill email_address from email
Phase 3: Update consumers to read email_address
Phase 4: Once all consumers updated, drop email
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. RENAME COLUMN is atomic but breaks any in-flight query referencing old name.
2. Add new column + trigger to keep in sync. Generated columns in PG 12+ simplify.
3. Backfill historical rows.
4. Update each consumer; track via grep + telemetry.
5. Drop old column after grace period.
rollback_procedure: Each phase reversible until DROP COLUMN
feature_flag_strategy: N/A — DB only
telemetry_requirements: Reads of old vs new column (via PostgreSQL views or app metrics)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Direct RENAME: prepared statements break; SQL across services breaks; outage.

title: Redux → Context with proper architectural rationale

current_state.description: App uses Redux for everything; bundle bloat, boilerplate
current_state.business_drivers: Bundle size complaints; team prefers less indirection

target_state.description: Context for cross-cutting; local state for components; React Query for server cache
target_state.benefits: Smaller bundle, less boilerplate, clearer state ownership

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Replace Redux globally without architectural rethink
No distinction between server cache and UI state
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Classify state: server cache vs UI state vs cross-cutting
Phase 2: Server cache → React Query / TanStack Query
Phase 3: UI state → useState/useReducer local
Phase 4: Cross-cutting (auth, theme) → focused Context
Phase 5: Remove Redux feature-by-feature
Phase 6: Remove redux dependency
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Redux often mixes server cache (which is async, has TTL, can be invalidated) with UI state (which is local, sync).
2. React Query for server cache: handles loading, error, refetch, dedupe.
3. Local state for component-local concerns.
4. Context for what's truly cross-cutting (auth user, theme).
5. Each feature migrated separately; Redux remains for un-migrated.
feature_flag_strategy: Feature-by-feature route; old Redux path until migrated
telemetry_requirements: Render counts; bundle size per release
what_breaks_naive_approach: Replace Redux with single Context: all consumers re-render on any change; perf worse than Redux.

title: Adding index online without locking

current_state.description: orders table 100M rows; need index on (customer_id, created_at) for new query
current_state.code_sample: -- CREATE INDEX idx ON orders(customer_id, created_at); -- blocks writes for hours

migration_strategy: Phase 1: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (online)
Phase 2: Monitor pg_stat_progress_create_index
Phase 3: Verify index valid (no errors)
Phase 4: ANALYZE to update planner stats
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY allows reads + writes during build.
2. Slower (does two passes) but no lock.
3. Can fail (e.g., constraint violation discovered late); leaves invalid index.
4. Monitor progress + status; drop invalid + retry if needed.
5. ANALYZE updates statistics so planner uses new index.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLY blocks writes for hours; 5xx errors flood.

title: Express 4 → 5 incremental

current_state.description: App on Express 4 with middleware that depends on changed behavior
current_state.code_sample: // async errors not caught by default in Express 4
current_state.business_drivers: Express 5 is stable; native async support, removed deprecated APIs

target_state.description: Express 5 with native async error handling
target_state.code_sample: app.get('/', async (req,res) => { throw new Error('caught natively'); });

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Audit dependencies on removed APIs (req.param, app.del, res.json with status arg)
Phase 2: Update code to non-deprecated patterns
Phase 3: Replace express-async-handler if used (no longer needed)
Phase 4: Upgrade package; run full test suite
Phase 5: Staging soak; canary in prod
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Breaking changes: removed req.param(), changed res.json signatures, routing pattern strictness.
2. Native async means thrown errors in handlers caught without wrapping.
3. Test suite is the safety net.
4. Canary: 1% prod traffic, monitor for 24h, ramp.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Blind upgrade: req.param() returns undefined, queries that worked silently fail.

title: Migrating MongoDB → PostgreSQL for orders

current_state.description: Orders in MongoDB; reporting needs SQL joins
current_state.business_drivers: Reporting team can't do joins; analytics suffers

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Postgres schema designed; deployed empty
Phase 2: App dual-writes (both Mongo + Postgres)
Phase 3: Bulk migrate historical from Mongo to Postgres
Phase 4: Reads gradually switch to Postgres (start with read-only reports)
Phase 5: All reads from Postgres
Phase 6: Stop writes to Mongo
Phase 7: Decommission Mongo
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Dual-write keeps systems in sync during migration.
2. Bulk migrate is idempotent (upsert by ID).
3. Reads switch gradually — start with non-critical paths.
4. Discrepancy detection: nightly job compares row counts + sample checksums.
5. Stop writes to old only after read switch proven.
6. Schema design: don't replicate Mongo's document shape; normalize.
feature_flag_strategy: Read path per-feature flag for Mongo vs Postgres
telemetry_requirements: Dual-write success rate; data divergence count; read latency per source
what_breaks_naive_approach: Big-bang switch: Mongo data not migrated, missing orders in Postgres, reporting wrong, customer complaints.

title: Replacing jQuery widgets with React, one widget at a time

current_state.description: Server-rendered HTML + jQuery widgets. Modernize incrementally.
current_state.business_drivers: Maintenance burden; can't hire jQuery talent

target_state.description: React mounted in islands replacing jQuery widgets

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Replace all widgets in one PR
migration_strategy: Phase 1: React build pipeline; mount script reads data-react-component attributes
Phase 2: Replace ONE widget — datepicker — both versions behind feature flag
Phase 3: A/B test (analytics consistent)
Phase 4: Roll out widget by widget
Phase 5: Eventually replace pages, not widgets
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Islands architecture: React mounted per element, not whole-page.
2. Server still renders shell; React enhances.
3. Each widget independent — no big-bang migration.
4. A/B prove equivalence before defaulting to new.
5. Eventually whole page becomes React — but earned, not forced.
feature_flag_strategy: data-react-datepicker-v2 vs jQuery default
telemetry_requirements: Widget render success; user interaction parity
what_breaks_naive_approach: SPA rewrite: 18 months, parity not achieved, executive impatience kills project.

title: Field-level deprecation with sunset header

current_state.description: Order response includes legacy `totalDollars` (float). Want to remove; clients should use `totalCents` (int).

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Both fields in response; deprecation header on field
Phase 2: Track per-client usage of totalDollars
Phase 3: Outreach to active consumers
Phase 4: Sunset date; remove
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Per-field deprecation requires consent from consumers.
2. Telemetry on which clients call requesting the deprecated field. Hard to detect on response fields — instead, ask clients to use Prefer header indicating which fields used.
3. Or remove in v2 of the endpoint.
feature_flag_strategy: Header-based opt-in
what_breaks_naive_approach: Silent removal: clients parsing totalDollars crash, support tickets flood.

title: Extracting a service: when to split databases

current_state.description: Inventory module heavily used by 5 services; tightly coupled DB schema
current_state.business_drivers: Inventory needs independent scale (Black Friday peak)

target_state.description: Inventory service with own DB; APIs to other services
target_state.code_sample: // orders calls inventory API for stock

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Synchronous cross-service calls in hot path
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Extract inventory module within monolith (test-003 pattern)
Phase 2: Add inventory service with own DB; backed by app-level dual-write
Phase 3: Backfill historical inventory data
Phase 4: Consumers call new service (with fallback to monolith)
Phase 5: Remove inventory tables from monolith DB
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Splitting DB before splitting service = distributed joins (bad).
2. Splitting service before DB = useful when DB writes still atomic but reads can scale.
3. Eventually consistent reads: introduce caching with stale-while-revalidate.
4. Joins replaced with N service calls — design APIs to batch (e.g., GET /inventory?skus=A,B,C).
5. Plan for partial failures with circuit breaker.
rollback_procedure: Per-phase revert; fallback to monolith if service down
feature_flag_strategy: Per-route: monolith vs service
telemetry_requirements: Latency; error rate; cache hit rate; circuit-breaker trips
what_breaks_naive_approach: Split with synchronous cross-service joins in hot path: latency explodes, cascading failures during peak.

title: Refactoring God class with extract-method

current_state.description: OrderProcessor 2000 lines, 47 methods, mixed concerns

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Characterization tests (migration-007)
Phase 2: Extract Method on logical chunks
Phase 3: Extract Class once methods cluster
Phase 4: Introduce Parameter Object for shared inputs
Phase 5: Dependency injection
Phase 6: Per-class unit tests
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Extract Method first — pulls logic into named functions without changing structure.
2. Once methods cluster by data, Extract Class.
3. DI enables testing.
4. Each step <100 lines diff; reviewable; revertable.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Rewrite: months of work, parity not achieved, original behaviors lost.

title: Mongoose 6 → 8 with discriminator changes

current_state.description: Discriminator schemas in Mongoose 6
current_state.code_sample: const Animal = mongoose.model('Animal', animalSchema); Dog = Animal.discriminator('Dog', dogSchema);

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Review CHANGELOG breaking changes
Phase 2: Bump to 7 first (intermediate)
Phase 3: Fix breaking changes
Phase 4: Bump to 8
Phase 5: Full test run + staging soak
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Stair-step major versions.
2. Discriminator options changed: defaults may differ.
3. Population options syntax changed.
4. Test coverage critical — schema changes can silently corrupt.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Direct jump 6→8: discriminator field changes; queries return wrong subclass.

title: Splitting a large table by partitioning

current_state.description: events table 500GB, queries slow

target_state.benefits: Faster queries; per-partition operations; easier archive

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Create new partitioned table (events_v2)
Phase 2: Create partitions for past + future
Phase 3: Backfill historical (chunked)
Phase 4: App dual-writes
Phase 5: Verify counts match
Phase 6: Rename atomically
Phase 7: Drop old
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Partitioning existing table = ALTER, takes hours, locks.
2. Create new partitioned, migrate, swap.
3. Each partition fits in memory better; queries pruned to relevant partitions.
4. Backups + maintenance per partition.
5. Plan partition naming + retention policy.
rollback_procedure: Rename back; old table preserved during transition
feature_flag_strategy: Reads from old until cutover
what_breaks_naive_approach: PARTITION BY in place: hours of locks; reads time out; cascading failures.

title: PII encryption migration (in-place re-encryption)

current_state.description: PII stored plaintext; needs encryption at rest

anti_patterns_to_avoid: Encrypting in place during write hot path
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Add ssn_enc column
Phase 2: App writes to BOTH plaintext + encrypted
Phase 3: Backfill encrypt historical
Phase 4: Reads gradually switch to encrypted
Phase 5: Stop writing plaintext
Phase 6: Securely wipe plaintext column
Phase 7: Validate no plaintext in backups beyond retention
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Encryption is one-way for queries (no LIKE on encrypted). Plan deterministic encryption for indexed fields if needed.
2. Backups contain plaintext until retention passes — coordinate with security team.
3. Key management: KMS for envelope encryption.
4. Audit log every read.
rollback_procedure: Dual-write phase reversible; after wipe, irreversible
feature_flag_strategy: Per-feature flag for plaintext vs encrypted read
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single UPDATE: locks, KMS rate limits, partial migration.

title: Callbacks → Promises → Async/Await

current_state.description: Node.js v0.10-era code with callback pyramids
current_state.business_drivers: Error handling buggy; new engineers slow to learn

migration_strategy: Phase 1: util.promisify for callback-based libs
Phase 2: Convert leaf functions first
Phase 3: Caller awaits
Phase 4: Top-level handlers with try/catch
Phase 5: ESLint rule blocks new callbacks
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Bottom-up: convert leaves first, callers cascade.
2. util.promisify wraps Node-callback-style; avoid manual new Promise.
3. Unhandled rejection logging at process level.
4. ESLint no-callback-literal + custom rules prevent regression.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: new Promise wrappers without proper reject; promises swallow errors; debugging hell.

title: Migrating REST → tRPC behind a gateway

current_state.description: REST API; TS clients lack type safety

target_state.description: tRPC routes; REST kept for non-TS consumers

migration_strategy: Phase 1: tRPC router alongside REST
Phase 2: TS clients migrate route by route
Phase 3: REST kept for external consumers
Phase 4: Deprecate REST when usage drops
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. tRPC + REST coexist; pick based on consumer.
2. tRPC procedures define types both server + client share.
3. REST endpoints kept for mobile, third parties.
4. Per-procedure migration; not all-or-nothing.
feature_flag_strategy: Route-level
what_breaks_naive_approach: Drop REST: external integrations break.

title: Foreign key addition on existing data

current_state.description: orders.customer_id references customers(id) — no FK

anti_patterns_to_avoid: ADD CONSTRAINT without NOT VALID (full table scan)
migration_strategy: Phase 1: Find orphans
Phase 2: Clean (delete or backfill)
Phase 3: ADD CONSTRAINT NOT VALID (fast, no scan)
Phase 4: VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (background, no block)
Phase 5: Verify validation succeeded
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. NOT VALID adds constraint without scanning; new rows enforced.
2. VALIDATE CONSTRAINT later, lock-free.
3. Orphans must be cleaned first or VALIDATE fails.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: ADD CONSTRAINT without NOT VALID locks for table scan; reads blocked.

title: Renaming an API field with backwards compatibility

current_state.description: Field `userId` should be `customerId`

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Response includes BOTH fields
Phase 2: Input accepts EITHER (with deprecation warning on old)
Phase 3: Track usage of old field
Phase 4: After grace period, remove from response
Phase 5: Remove from input
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Backwards-compat means accept both for input, return both for output.
2. Deprecation header.
3. Output bloat is real cost; balance.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single rename: integrations break.

title: Node.js LTS jump 18 → 20

current_state.description: App on Node 18; deps EOL in 6 months

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Review Node 19/20 deprecations
Phase 2: CI matrix runs on both
Phase 3: Fix incompatibilities
Phase 4: Staging on new for soak
Phase 5: Canary prod 1% → 100%
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Test matrix (test-186) gates merges.
2. Performance benchmark before/after.
3. Native module rebuild for new ABI.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
solution:
# Dockerfile change
# FROM node:18-alpine
FROM node:20-alpine
# Rebuild native deps in CI

# CI matrix
# strategy: matrix: node: ['18.x', '20.x']

# Performance bench script
# scripts/perf-bench.sh
what_breaks_naive_approach: Direct upgrade: native deps fail; deprecation warnings flood logs.

title: Replace conditional with polymorphism

current_state.description: if-else chain on type: 12 branches, hard to extend
current_state.business_drivers: Adding type C requires touching all switch sites

target_state.description: Strategy pattern; new types add a class

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Tests for current behavior
Phase 2: Extract each branch to function
Phase 3: Map type → function
Phase 4: Promote to class hierarchy if behavior shares state
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Open/closed: add behavior without modifying existing.
2. Use polymorphism when branches grow > 3-4.
3. Map of functions is lightest weight.
4. Class hierarchy when state shared.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
telemetry_requirements: Emit rollout, error-rate, latency, adoption, rollback, and user-impact metrics with enough tags to compare old and new paths.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Long if-else chains rot; new types miss branches; bugs.

title: Time-zone aware migration: storing UTC vs local

current_state.description: created_at stored as local time without TZ

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Add new TIMESTAMPTZ column
Phase 2: Backfill assuming original TZ (document assumption)
Phase 3: App writes both
Phase 4: Verify equivalence
Phase 5: Drop old
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Assumption: original timestamps were in server TZ (e.g., UTC or America/New_York).
2. Backfill: cast naive timestamp AT TIME ZONE 'original_tz'.
3. Verify random samples manually.
4. App layer: always send TZ-aware.
feature_flag_strategy: Read flag
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cast wrong TZ: data interpretations all shift; subtle wrong.

title: Event bus for async cross-service communication

current_state.description: Services synchronously call each other; cascading failures

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Event bus deployed
Phase 2: Identify async-eligible flows
Phase 3: Producers publish events alongside existing calls
Phase 4: Consumers subscribe
Phase 5: Remove synchronous calls
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Events for things that are eventually consistent.
2. Synchronous for true read-after-write requirements.
3. Schema registry (Avro / JSON Schema) prevents breaking changes.
4. Replay capability via topic retention.
rollback_procedure: Disable subscribers; fall back to sync
feature_flag_strategy: Per-event consumer toggle
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tight coupling: one service down crashes others.

title: Migrating from server-rendered templates to React SSR

current_state.description: Express + EJS templates

migration_strategy: Phase 1: Next.js deployed on different subdomain or path
Phase 2: Reverse-proxy routes some paths to Next
Phase 3: Migrate pages individually
Phase 4: Shared session via cookie
Phase 5: Eventually Next becomes primary
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Per-route migration; both apps live.
2. Reverse proxy is the seam.
3. Shared auth via cookie domain.
4. Don't try to share components across both stacks during transition.
feature_flag_strategy: Per-path
what_breaks_naive_approach: Big rewrite: SEO drops; auth bugs; users lost.

title: JavaScript → TypeScript incremental adoption

current_state.description: 100k LOC JS; no types

target_state.code_sample: function f(x: Foo): string { return x.foo; }

migration_strategy: Phase 1: tsconfig with allowJs, checkJs=false, strict=false
Phase 2: Rename leaf modules .js → .ts
Phase 3: Add types; fix errors
Phase 4: Enable noImplicitAny per file via // @ts-check
Phase 5: Enable strict gradually
Phase 6: Remove allowJs
reasoning:
Senior thinking:
1. Per-file migration: leaves first.
2. // @ts-check enables checking in JS files (transitional).
3. types-only PRs separated from feature PRs.
4. strict flag enabled file-by-file via @ts-strict.
5. Patience: 100k LOC = 6-12 months.
feature_flag_strategy: Ship behind a reversible flag with owner, expiry date, cohort targeting, metrics, and an emergency disable path exercised before rollout.
what_breaks_naive_approach: ts-migrate bulk: thousands of any; type checks meaningless; no improvement.
